policy_statement: Sustainably manage and recover 200,000 hectares of native forests, delivering around 0.9–1.2 MtCO2eq annual GHG captures by 2030 (I4).
mechanism_description: Chile commits to sustainably manage and recover native forests to increase annual GHG captures through enhanced carbon sequestration.
primary_causal_pathway: Ecosystem restoration/management → ↑ biomass & soil carbon storage → ↑ CO2 sequestration (net removals) → ↓ atmospheric CO2
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Human activity/system changed: forest governance and on-the-ground practices (restoration, regeneration support, reduced degradation, sustainable harvesting where applicable) across native forest areas.  
(2) Mediating process: increased photosynthetic uptake and accumulation of carbon in living biomass, deadwood/litter, and soils; reduced disturbance/degradation helps maintain and grow carbon stocks.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: higher net carbon sinks (annual MtCO2e captured), lowering net GHG emissions through removals.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is increased net removal of atmospheric CO2 via enhanced carbon sequestration in restored/managed native forests.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: ecosystem restoration/management increases carbon stocks, producing net CO2 removals—this is the primary climate effect across policies using this mechanism.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: while forest restoration can also improve resilience (e.g., watershed regulation, reduced erosion) and resource outcomes (e.g., timber efficiency), the mechanism as defined is explicitly about increasing GHG captures via sequestration, making the nature-based carbon sink pathway dominant.  
3) Edge cases applied: if a “management” policy primarily increased harvest yields or substituted wood for more carbon-intensive materials, the dominant pathway could shift toward mitigation via substitution or resource efficiency; however, that would be a different mechanism than “carbon sequestration enhancement.”  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: any policy whose core mechanism is native forest restoration/management intended to enhance sequestration shares the same primary nature-based removals pathway.  
5) Secondary subtype deferral: any additional categorization (e.g., adaptation co-benefits, biodiversity goals, fire-risk management specifics) must be assigned at the individual policy level based on explicit evidence, not assumed from the mechanism alone.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Potential edge cases include (a) restoration that is primarily for adaptation (flood/heat risk) with sequestration incidental, or (b) “sustainable management” that increases harvesting and reduces net carbon stocks; those would warrant a different mechanism/primary classification if the dominant effect is not net sequestration.
key_indicators: “commits”; “sustainable management and recovery”; “200,000 hectares of native forests”; “GHG captures of around 0.9 to 1.2 MtCO2eq annually”; “by 2030”
co_benefits: Biodiversity conservation; watershed protection and water regulation; soil conservation/erosion control; wildfire risk reduction (potentially); livelihoods/community benefits from sustainable forest management.
instance_edge_case_notes: No instrument implementation details are provided (e.g., subsidies, regulation, protected areas, MRV), so classification is limited to a quantified commitment/goal (target-setting) rather than an incentive or regulatory instrument.
